metadata.seed: Well, just kind of. Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.

patient.query: Well, just kind of. Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.

automatic_thoughts: I don't know what I want to do
business isn't for me
I feel like I don't have a purpose
I'm just living off my parents' money

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're carrying a heavy burden of feeling lost and unsure of your path forward. It's understandable that you're feeling this way, especially when you're questioning your purpose and feeling tied to your parents' support. What would it be like to imagine a future where you felt more in control of your own direction, even if it's just a small step?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel heavy. Sometimes I just wake up and wonder if I'm even on the right track. Imagining a future where I feel more in control is nice, but then I think about all the risks. What if I make the wrong choice and it just ends up being another dead end?

automatic_thoughts: wonder if I'm on the right track
what if I make the wrong choice
it just ends up being another dead end

llm_response.response: It sounds like those thoughts can be quite overwhelming, especially when you're just starting your day. What makes you believe that making the "wrong" choice would necessarily lead to a dead end?

patient.query: I guess it’s just something I have in my mind from past experiences. I've tried different paths before, and sometimes it felt like I just wasted time. I don’t want to feel stuck again, you know? It's like every decision feels so crucial, and I worry I’ll mess it up again.

automatic_thoughts: I just wasted time
I don’t want to feel stuck
every decision feels crucial
I’ll mess it up again

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot of weight from those past experiences, and it's understandable that you'd want to avoid feeling stuck again. Perhaps you could consider if there's another way to view those past choices – maybe they offered valuable lessons even if they didn't lead where you initially hoped?

patient.query: Yeah, I see what you mean. I guess some of those experiences did teach me things, but it’s hard to focus on that when I’m caught up in the feeling of failure. It’s like I can't shake the idea that I should’ve done better or chosen differently. I just don’t want to repeat those mistakes.

automatic_thoughts: I should’ve done better
I should've chosen differently
I don't want to repeat those mistakes

llm_response.response: It sounds like the feeling of failure is really dominating your thoughts right now, making it difficult to see any positives. Those "should've" thoughts can be incredibly persistent and create a lot of pressure. Let’s explore what’s fueling those thoughts and whether they’re truly helpful in moving forward.

patient.query: Yeah, they really do take over sometimes. It’s like I'm constantly comparing myself to others, and then I just feel like I'm falling short. I guess I thought I’d be in a better place by now, but instead, I just feel stuck. It’s frustrating, and it makes me doubt myself even more.

automatic_thoughts: I'm falling short
I thought I’d be in a better place by now
I doubt myself

llm_response.response: It sounds like these comparisons can be quite overwhelming, and it’s understandable that you’d feel frustrated when things don’t go as planned. What makes you feel like you're falling short when you compare yourself to others?

patient.query: It’s just... I see people moving forward, getting promotions or finding jobs they love, and I can’t help but think, “Why am I not there yet?” It makes me feel like I’m not working hard enough or that I’m somehow less capable. I guess deep down, I worry that I’ll never catch up or be good enough.

automatic_thoughts: Why am I not there yet?
I'm not working hard enough
I'm somehow less capable
I'll never catch up
I'll never be good enough

llm_response.response: It sounds like seeing others progress can bring up a lot of difficult feelings. It’s understandable to question where you are in comparison and wonder if you’re on the right track. What makes you think you’re not working hard enough?

patient.query: Yeah, it’s tough. Sometimes I just feel like I’m going through the motions, you know? I put in hours, and yet when I look at the results—or lack of results—I can't help but think I could be doing more. I guess I just expect myself to always be at my best, and when I'm not, it’s... disappointing. It makes me doubt if I’m really cut out for this.

automatic_thoughts: I could be doing more
I expect myself to always be at my best
I'm not cut out for this

llm_response.response:
REASONING:
The patient is expressing feelings of disappointment and self-doubt after not achieving the results they expected. Their automatic thought, "I expect myself to always be at my best," seems to be a core belief driving this distress. This belief is rigid and likely contributes to their feelings of inadequacy. Applying the cognitive restructuring technique will help the patient examine this expectation and consider alternative perspectives.

FINAL RESPONSE:
It sounds like you're experiencing a real sense of letdown when things don't go as planned. It takes courage to acknowledge those feelings and to be honest about the self-doubt that arises. Perhaps we could explore where that expectation of always being at your best comes from, and whether it's truly serving you.

patient.query: I appreciate that. I know I put a lot of pressure on myself, and sometimes it feels like I have to prove I’m worthy of my job or my place in things. I guess it’s hard to shake that feeling, like if I’m not perfect, then I’m not enough. But then I also wonder if I’m being too hard on myself... I just don’t want to let anyone down.

automatic_thoughts: I have to prove I’m worthy of my job
If I’m not perfect, then I’m not enough
I might be too hard on myself

llm_response.response:
REASONING:
The patient is expressing a sense of pressure and inadequacy, linking their worth to performance and fearing disappointing others. The automatic thought "if I’m not perfect, then I’m not enough" is a core belief driving this distress. This belief appears rigid and limiting, suggesting that cognitive restructuring would be a helpful intervention. The patient's self-questioning ("I wonder if I’m being too hard on myself") indicates a potential openness to exploring alternative perspectives.

FINAL RESPONSE:
It sounds like you're carrying a heavy burden, feeling the need to constantly demonstrate your value. It takes courage to acknowledge that you might be holding yourself to an incredibly high standard. Perhaps we could explore where that standard comes from and whether it truly serves you.

patient.query: I think you're right; it does feel like a weight sometimes. I guess, looking back, there were always these expectations—maybe from my family or even just what I see in society. It makes sense to want to do well, but then it can feel crushing too. I’m just scared that if I let go of those standards, I’ll lose my drive or something. But maybe I am just running myself ragged trying to keep up.

automatic_thoughts: it feels like a weight
if I let go of standards, I'll lose my drive
I'm running myself ragged

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're recognizing a real struggle between wanting to meet those expectations and the toll it's taking on you. What makes you think that letting go of those standards would necessarily mean losing your drive? Perhaps there's a different way to define or maintain motivation.

patient.query: Yeah, I do feel that tension. I guess it’s just this fear that without those expectations, I might lose focus or get lazy. It's like I’ve built this identity around being the "hard worker" or the "achiever," and if that slips away, what do I have left? But at the same time, I know it’s exhausting trying to hold onto that all the time. It’s just... complicated, you know?

automatic_thoughts: without expectations, I might lose focus or get lazy
I've built my identity around being the 'hard worker' or 'achiever'
if that slips away, what do I have left?

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're caught between wanting to let go of the pressure and worrying about who you'd be without it. It makes sense that you'd feel complicated when something so central to your sense of self is being questioned. Perhaps we could explore what else makes you, *you*, beyond just being a hard worker or achiever?
